data.title: LG Display: Results Beat Reinforces My Bullish View
data.description:
LG Display Co., Ltd. (LPL) (034220.KS) is still rated as Buy. LPL's margin outlook and Q1 financials were detailed in my earlier April 28, 2025 piece.

Its Q2 performance announced today (July 24) surpassed expectations, and I think that the positive momentum is sustainable for the quarter ahead.

Firm Has Become Profitable Again In Most Recent Period

LPL was loss-making for 11 of the past twelve quarters up till March 31, 2025.

Analysts had feared the worst, with the consensus projections pointing to a -₩173B (source: S&P Capital IQ) net loss in the April-June '25 time frame.

My late-April write-up predicted that the group "will be in the black this year." I proved to be right, as it registered a Q2'25 net profit attributable to stockholders of +KRW 0.87T.

OLED (Organic Light-Emitting Diode)-related solutions as a proportion of its sales went up +400 bps (basis points) YoY to 56% in the second quarter of the current year.

The company has been migrating away from low-priced products in the mature LCD (Liquid Crystal Display) market. In its earnings slides, LPL drew attention to the "LCD business exit" and "OLED TV panel shipment increase."

This paid off with its Q2'25 selling price on a per-cubic meter basis surging +36% year-on-year and +31% sequentially.

Separately, LPL's depreciation expenses declined -15% YoY to ₩988 billion for the recent quarter.

In my April 28 article, I noted that "two of LPL's older production plants will reach their respective salvage values" this year, and it should "conclude the divestiture of an LCD facility in China" by Q2. These favorable developments have contributed to a lower carrying value for its properties.

Also, LPL saw the net debt-to-equity metric improve 19 ppts (percentage points) QoQ to 155% as of June 30, 2025. This would have eased its financial burden.

The firm's net borrowings were reduced by a meaningful KRW 1,841B in the past three months, which has enhanced its profitability.

LPL's results were boosted by an advantageous shift in topline composition, a drop in certain non-variable costs, and a strengthening of its balance sheet.

I'm Anticipating That The Group's Future Will Be Even Brighter

LPL is at the early stages of its margin recovery journey.

Some of the tailwinds it experienced last quarter haven't reached full potential.

The company previously took part in Bank of America's (BAC) late-May Korean investment forum. BAC issued a report (not publicly available) titled "2025 Korea Conference" on May 21 outlining LPL's disclosures. A takeaway is LPL's goal to have OLED-linked offerings account for over three-fifths (vs 56% in 2Q) of its revenue, as this screen technology "becomes mainstream," in the management's words.

The OLED penetration rate for vehicle displays worldwide is forecast to rise from a mere 0.1% three years ago to 4.8% and 8.9% in 2025 and 2026, respectively, as per TrendForce's analysis.

LPL's share of top line generated from auto-related products has also increased from 8% for Q2'24 and 9% for Q1'25 to Q2 2025's 10%.

You can imagine that the firm's "Average Selling Price" or "ASP" will expand further, as its sales mix optimization still has legs to run.

Considering market trends and internal targets mentioned above, revenues from its high-priced OLED-focused (versus LCD) product range should continue to grow.

Also, there's still more fixed-cost savings in store for LPL.

LPL had guided for depreciation expenses amounting to around ₩4T for full-year FY25 at the BAC event referred to earlier. This translates into an implied decline of -6%.

Its depreciation-related costs have already fallen by -13% YoY in the first half of the year. The firm is on track to meet or even exceed its guidance. It has a major (60k-sheet monthly capacity) fab in Guangzhou, China, that should be fully depreciated by 2H25 based on my estimates.

It's worth noting LPL's interest cost downtrend as well.

As the group turns earnings-positive again, it would have room to repay a greater amount of debt going forward. The benchmark rate in LPL's home market, Korea, was also cut by a cumulative 50 bps since the beginning of the year.

My crystal ball tells me that LPL's bottom-line will more than double QoQ to KRW 180B in Q3'25 after considering the margin drivers discussed here. A YoY comparison isn't appropriate, as it was in the red for the same period last year.

You Would Contemplate These Downside Risks

A slower-than-expected increase in OLED adoption rate could lead to ASP and margin disappointments for the company.

If LPL chooses to ramp up capital expenditures, this would entail higher-than-anticipated depreciation expenses in the future.

My Closing Thoughts

LPL remains a buy-rated name.

The last time the firm was profitable on a full-year basis was in FY21. During that period, the shares didn't trade below 0.22 times price-to-sales. LPL's capital appreciation potential is at least +22% justifying a buy. This assumes that its P/S multiple expands from the current 0.18x to that level.

Its ongoing profitability improvement should act as a powerful re-rating trigger.

data.title: SCHI: Intermediate Corporate Bond Outlook
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyse the Schwab 5-10 Year Corporate Bond (NYSEARCA:SCHI) investment vehicle in the exchange-traded fund (ETF) category, managed by the investment firm Charles Schwab. This vehicle seeks to passively replicate the Bloomberg US 5-10 Year Corporate Bond Index, before commissions and expenses. This index measures the performance of investment-grade bonds issued in the US, with a minimum outstanding value of $300 million and maturities between 5 and 10 years.

To replicate the Bloomberg index, the ETF follows a resampling strategy, selecting a representative sample of the entire basket of bonds held by the index. Currently, it invests at least 90% of its assets under management in this basket, leaving (if it does so) the other 10% in cash or hedging of some kind. This approach allows the vehicle to reduce the transaction costs it incurs and maintain a very low tracking error relative to the index.

As can be seen in the image below, the ETF portfolio is composed of 2,236 different bonds, which provides it with a high degree of diversification by issuer and sector. The portfolio's turnover rate is 21.17% per annum, which indicates that it makes certain periodic adjustments, but not excessive ones, in the selection of bonds. This level of turnover suggests that, despite the passive approach, the fund managers make regular adjustments to maintain index tracking, optimize transaction costs and ensure alignment with the maturity and rating characteristics required by their investors.

From a maturity perspective, the fund has a weighted average maturity ratio of 7.44 years and an effective duration of 6.1 years. Maturity indicates that the vehicle's portfolio is in the middle of the bond spectrum, with maturities between 5 and 10 years, while duration measures the fund's sensitivity to interest rate changes. In this case, the fund has a medium level of risk with respect to these variations, as a 1% increase in rates would cause the fund to fall by 6.1% with its current average duration.

With regard to the coupon, the bonds that make up the fund offer an average annual coupon yield of 4.67%. This periodic interest flow is what, after deducting the meagre 0.03% in expenses, is distributed monthly to the participants. Finally, the historical volatility (three-year standard deviation) of the vehicle stands at 8.34%, a level typical of intermediate corporate fixed income, which is sufficient to provide returns in an expanding economic cycle.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first metric we will analyse today is the Butterfly Heatmap, for the 3-5-7, 5-7-10 and 7-10-20 year periods respectively. What this metric does is basically measure the curvature of the different curves constructed with the aforementioned time periods. As the fund concentrates on bond maturities in its portfolio in the 5 to 10-year period, with an effective duration of 6.1, we have chosen the three tranches mentioned above. Readers may wonder how and to what extent this influences their investment. We will discuss this below.

Given these characteristics, the fund under analysis is greatly affected by variations in the yield curve. When the butterfly is greater than zero, in the (5-7-10) segment, it means that the 7-year bond yields more than the 5- and 10-year bonds. In this environment, the ETF can benefit from an appreciation in the price of these bonds if the average maturity (7) of the curve returns to zero.

This phenomenon can be caused by imbalances in supply and demand due to maturity. If the market believes that the Fed's interest rate policy will have a greater impact on short or long terms, it may overbuy those tranches (5 and 10 years), putting downward pressure on their yields, while the middle tranche is relatively neglected and its required coupon rises. Companies with their own issues may also have an influence. Sometimes they prefer to issue in intermediate maturities (6-8 years) to avoid the cost of longer-term bonds, which increases supply in the 7-year tranche and raises its yield.

In the second metric, we show the z-score of the butterfly calculated over the trio of years (5-7-10). The z-score is a statistical measure that tells us how many deviations the constructed yield curve is moving away from or not moving away from its mean; in short, it helps us measure the degree of curvature of the yield curve itself. As can be seen in the image, we have converted the metric into a kind of trading signal for the aforementioned time period.

When the z-score is greater than +2 (red dot), the graph indicates that the midpoint of the range (7y) is excessively expensive compared to its wings (5y, 10y). This situation usually precedes a compression of the butterfly's curvature, which will cause 7-year bonds to fall in yield and rise in price, benefiting the vehicle analysed and which we take as a sell signal.

On the other hand, if the z-score is less than -2 (green dot), the opposite occurs: the middle section becomes extremely cheap (very low yield). In this case, the curve is likely to ‘widen’ again, bringing the butterfly towards zero from below, and therefore 7-year yields will rise (prices will fall). In this case, for ETF managers, this would be a signal to buy or slightly increase duration, as these intermediate bonds are ‘undervalued’.

In corporate fixed income markets, investors demand an additional premium over Treasury bonds to assume credit, liquidity and term risks. By calculating Z-scores on each of these spreads—that is, standardising their value against their performance over the last 21 sessions—we can identify moments when the market exceptionally penalises or rewards corporate issues. A high Z-score (above +2) indicates that the spread is at relative highs, and a very low Z-score (below –2) signals conditions of extreme premium compression.

In the case of this ETF, which groups together more than 2,200 medium-term investment-grade bonds, credit spreads directly reflect the perception of credit risk in the American corporate universe. When these spreads shoot up (red sections on the heat map), bond prices fall and, therefore, the fund's NAV suffers. However, an excessive spike in spreads is usually followed by stabilisation or partial reversal, providing opportunities for our liability manager to revalue.

The liquidity premium captures the extra cost that investors demand to buy or sell less-traded bonds. In times of financial stress — when the liquidity Z-score rises above 2 — spreads generate an additional drag on coupons, because spreads widen and depress prices.

Conclusion

In conclusion and to end this article, my recommendation is to keep the vehicle in the portfolio, as the three metrics analysed point to an intermediate market at this time. The tactical opportunity to capture compression or expansion of curvature and spreads has largely been exploited, and the indicators are moving in neutral zones. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Robinhood: The Next Phase Of Fintech Begins (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since I initiated coverage on Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD), the stock has delivered an extraordinary 280% price return, far outpacing the S&P 500’s ~9% price gain. The thesis proved effective, driven by Robinhood’s expanding base of funded accounts and surging Assets Under Custody (AUC), which acted as key topline growth catalysts. That analysis was focused on Robinhood's appeal to younger investors that may derive stable top-line growth.

Today, the thesis has expanded to add more on a diversified ecosystem that includes wealth management, crypto, and tokenized equities. Robinhood's aggressive product expansion and operational leverage now push it as a high-growth fintech capable of maintaining 33% to 46% topline growth in upcoming quarters amid regulatory headwinds.

High Revenue Growth Through Diversified Ecosystem

Robinhood Markets has an aggressive product diversification and expansion of revenue in progress that continue to boost its stock price growth. The company has evolved from a commission-free trading platform into a financial ecosystem. Robinhood is integrating new offerings like futures, prediction markets, tokenized stocks, cryptocurrency trading, banking services, credit cards, and wealth management through the acquisition of TradePMR. This strategic diversification reduces Robinhood’s dependence on volatile trading revenues and scaled up customer wallet share.

The company targets to capture a broader market, including active traders, high-net-worth individuals, and international customers. For instance, in Q1-FY25, revenues reached $927 million with a 50% YoY increase that far surpassed the sector median revenue growth of 6.27%. The trend may continue in the upcoming quarters. Over the trailing twelve months (TTM), total revenues hit $3.3 billion, with a 59.65% YoY growth rate. This is highlighting Robinhood’s rapid scaling against its peers.

The topline growth is based on double-digit increases in transaction volumes across all major asset classes. These include an 84% YoY surge in equities trading, a record quarter for options trading, and $260 million in crypto revenue. The introduction of new products like futures, index options, and prediction markets, each contributing ~$20 million in annual recurring revenue (ARR). This points to the leads of the diversification strategy.

Additionally, the acquisition of TradePMR brought $40 billion in platform assets and a $50 million run-rate business. Whereas Robinhood Strategies, launched in March 2025, attracted over 40,000 customers and $100 million in assets within weeks. In short, Robinhood is quickly scaling new offerings, leveraging its existing user base of over 25 million funded accounts to derive adoption.

Robinhood’s financials provide more details of its diversification strategy’s outcomes. The company hit a 72% incremental adjusted EBITDA margin in Q1-FY25, with the overall adjusted EBITDA margin expanding by 11 points YoY to 51%. This reflects solid operating leverage as topline growth outpaces expense growth, with adjusted operating expenses and stock-based compensation (OpEx and SBC) at $533 million.

These are well within the company’s full-year guidance of $2.085-$2.185 billion. The growth in high-margin businesses like Robinhood Gold subscriptions that nearly doubled YoY to 3.3 million subscribers by April 2025 (a 12% adoption rate overall and 33% among new Q1 customers), provides a stable topline that mitigates the volatility of trading-based income.

Similarly, Interest-earning assets that include $8.4 billion in margin balances (up 2x YoY) and $30.8 billion in cash sweep balances (up 52% YoY), further boost profitability and offset lower-margin trading activities. As a result, the 1117.91% YoY EPS diluted growth against the sector median of 8.46% points to the profitability impact of these diverse toplines.

Moreover, Robinhood’s focus on active traders through platforms like Robinhood Legend (had strong incremental volume growth) and futures trading (with 4.5 million contracts traded in April 2025) ensures stability against market volatility. The company’s retirement assets, now at $16 billion (up 20% YTD), and the integration of TradePMR’s wealth management capabilities push Robinhood to capitalize on the wealth transfer to younger generations (a demographic in line with its user base).

Internationally, Robinhood’s 150,000+ customers and the pending Bitstamp acquisition, set to close mid-2025, add more revenue growth potential. Bitstamp has ~500,000 funded customers that may boost Robinhood’s crypto trading volumes. $18 billion in net deposits in Q1 and $28 billion year-to-date through May reflects an 18% annualized growth rate relative to April platform assets of $255 billion (up 89% YoY).

In short, these metrics highlight solid customer engagement and trust as users put more assets in Robinhood’s expanding ecosystem. With that, the company is cross-selling products, with Gold subscribers 4x more likely to hold retirement accounts and 5x more likely to have higher assets, further scale up revenue growth potential. By diversifying its offerings, Robinhood captures a larger share of customer financial activity and builds a stable business model that supports topline growth over the mid-to-long term.

With ~800% Valuation Premium, Regulatory Risks Loom

On the downside, Robinhood’s approach to financial services is attached with regulatory scrutiny and compliance risks (particularly in cryptocurrency and tokenized stocks). This weakness also supports business growth by pushing Robinhood as a fintech pioneer. The legal issues may attract attention from tech-savvy investors and require operational improvements that may boost long-term scalability as a result. The Bank of Lithuania’s scrutiny regarding Robinhood’s tokenized equity tokens for OpenAI and SpaceX points to the issue.

These tokens (launched in June 2025) cover over 200 U.S. stocks and ETFs on the Arbitrum blockchain and have plans for a custom Ethereum-based Layer 2 blockchain. The move supported a 173% year-to-date (YTD) stock price rally with shares reaching ~$110 by July reflecting street excitement for blockchain-based finance. However, the regulatory inquiry led to a share price drop, and OpenAI’s public denial of affiliation with these tokens (stating they do not represent real equity and lack approval for equity transfers) reflects major compliance risks.

This scrutiny extends to Robinhood’s crypto operations, with $11.7 billion in crypto trading volume in May 2025 (up 65% YoY) and $260 million in Q1 crypto revenue boosted by the pending Bitstamp acquisition. The acquisition introduces additional regulatory complexity under frameworks like the EU’s Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) regulation.

These regulatory issues are reflected in Robinhood’s valuation metrics with a current P/E GAAP of 58x which is 320% above the sector median of 13.8x and a Price/Sales of 28x, 774% above the sector median of 3.2x, pointing to a premium valuation attached with possible regulatory setbacks. The share price drop following OpenAI’s statement reflects the market sensitivity to compliance issues.

Overall, the regulatory environment pushes Robinhood to make its Compliance structure more robust that will instill confidence with Institutional and High-Net-Worth clients in alignment with its 10-year vision of a global financial ecosystem. Also, Robinhood’s advocacy for regulatory clarity like revising accredited investor rules and supporting crypto asset securities push it towards new opportunities like crypto staking that awaits SEC guidance.

Lastly, the company’s engagement with policymakers signals progress toward a favorable regulatory environment that can potentially enable tokenization of private company stocks like SpaceX and OpenAI. This could democratize access to high-growth investments in line with Robinhood’s mission and derive user growth. The 150,000+ international customers and Bitstamp’s global presence reflects this potential. Moving forward, regulatory resolutions can accelerate international adoption and boost the stock price up (news-wise).

Takeaway

Robinhood is evolving into a bigger fintech platform with strong growth drivers. There are still some risk factors related to regulation, but product expansion, widening margins, and adoption of users indicate more room for long-term investors. Its transition to wealth management and crypto infrastructure could see more revenue streams materialise and more customer loyalty in the long-term.

data.title: Warden Capital Q2 2025 Letter
data.description:
Here we are, another year older and hopefully wiser. I wanted to share thoughts on the current state of affairs as well as reflections on the prior year & some thoughts on moving forward in 2025. As per usual I intend do a CRE sector specific write up later once all the year end results are in.

As always, this is not investing advice and please read the general disclaimer, here.

First, the returns. We had a solid year - we returned 8.48%, vs 7.60% for our real estate benchmark, leaping from underperformance most of the year back to outperforming on a strong (relative) Q4. When compared to the S&P’s high return of over 25%, it doesn’t feel quite as good, but we did significantly outperform the S&P the past two years, and you can’t win every single year.

Since inception we are still handily outperforming the REIT index, and also the S&P, with returns of 189.92% (about 25.5% annualized), vs 64.93% for the USRT index (about 11.3% annualized) & 121.20% for the S&P (about 18.5% annualized).

I feel especially good about the fact we are beating the S&P since inception considering that we have zero exposure to the mag 7, which is what is driving essentially all the returns in that index (and big tech in general really).

To illustrate here is an interesting chart from the FT showing the increasing concentration in the S&P.

FT article link here

Of the top 10 stocks, all but1 , Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B), are tech related. Now in fairness, big tech has had a magnificent run of generating increasing profits and cash flows, so to some degree it is natural we have this level of concentration. The risk in my view is that if the growth slows or stops, we come crashing back down to reality as the market is increasingly tied to the fortunes of a handful of firms.

And what is driving big tech’s growth? Well over the last decade it was ‘software eating the world’ as Marc Andreesen famously predicted. However past is not necessarily predictive of future, and things look a bit different looking forward vs back.

Now - I’m not a big tech expert, but I do feel obligated to stay somewhat on top of the goings on there simply due to the aforementioned massive economic concentration (and CRE generally gains on population growth and income growth, big tech being a huge driver of the latter).

As I’m sure you are already aware, AI has been and continues to be all over the news, specifically the subset called generative AI, powered by large language models or LLMs mostly. The recent advances in this field have unleashed an investment boom of historic proportions, largely in the data center and semi-conductors world.1

How big is it? Well it looks like based on estimates from economist Skanda Amarnath (full article viewable here on Bloomberg), we are getting close to 2000 levels for telecom & data centers.

This is especially relevant, because the capex spend relating to AI on data centers, chips and training / inference time seems to have dramatically outstripped actual AI end user spending.

I had trouble finding good estimates for either of these things, but it looks like there is at least ~$200-220 billion in direct AI related chips and data center spending, if not more (Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom's (AVGO) AI products are ~$150b alone).2

However the end user spending on AI services is much smaller, albeit growing quickly. How much smaller? Well again this is very difficult to find as many firms do not disclose the specifics, but it looks like total spend in 2024 could easily be under $15 billion.

Menlo Equities, a reputable VC firm, seems to estimate enterprise end use spending (so not the capex models / training / servers part) at $4.6 billion for 2024, but up significantly from 2023.

On the consumer side - Open AI apparently has a run rate around $5b. If we generously assume there is another $6b of consumer and unaccounted for enterprise spend, we get to maybe $16b of revenues, against capex of probably $220 billion.

Even at a generous 30% profit margin (which is absurd as Open AI lost $5 billion to generate their ~$5 billion in revenues), that’s $5.3b of actual return on $220b of spend, an awful ROI of just ~2.5%! When you further consider much of this stuff has a very short useful life, the ROI looks even worse.

The only way this makes sense is if end use for AI continues to grow at a massive pace for many years. So the next question would be, how much end user spending would we need to justify the capex wave? Well given the useful life of much of this capex (at least the chip part), is a measly ~5 years3 , this means you’d likely want a ~30% return on the spend. If we assume some things like data center power/cooling are longer lived and we use 10 years of average useful life, we could conservatively look for a 20% return. That would be ~$44 billion, or nearly 3x the total current industry revenues! But again revenue is not profit, so again using an extremely generous 30% margin, that is ~$145 billion in needed revenues, or ~10x current levels. At a 20% margin its $220 billion, and 10% its $440!4

And this is to say nothing of potential improvements in AI model efficiency, which is very relevant as Chinese firm Deepseek (DEEPSEEK) recently released a GPT4 analog model in December and an o1 reasoning type model just the other week. Deepseek’s model is open source, and it appears to be massively more efficient than the leading American models, seemingly due in no small part due to the chip constraints Deepseek had to operate under. Estimates vary but the simplest comparison to me is their price per 1mm tokens (tokens represent model output essentially) - Deepseek is almost 30x cheaper in this regard!

This is a seismic shift in pricing if it holds. To match that kind of pricing would knock down industry revenues from ~$15B to ~$0.5B! Sure utilization will increase with lower prices, but honestly I don’t think pricing is a huge holdup right now for many users. Most of ChatGPT’s revenue appears to come from its basic $20/mo subscription - this isn’t exactly terribly expensive. If you drop that to just a dollar a month I’m sure they would get more users, but I don’t think it would be 20x. 2-4x seems more realistic at best case. So conceivably the industry revenue could drop to something like $1-2 billion in a worst case scenario - I don’t think this is likely but it illustrates the scale of the problem.

Will AI end user spending grow in 2025? Very likely yes.5,6 Will it continue strong growth thereafter? I would also bet yes. But will there be a sufficient return to justify the current massive capital spending? It seems very unlikely to me, especially now with Deepseek’s massively lower pricing.7 What feels more and more likely is a cousin of what happened in 2000 - hype and mania lead to overspending on capital, creating poor returns for the investors but dramatically reducing cost of the physical side of things, allowing innovation to continue at a nice clip. The billion dollar question is, is this 1999 or 1996 as relates to AI capex spend?

As I wrote this piece originally I was leaning more 1996, however the release was delayed awhile because I got sick. During that time we had OpenAI announce its teaming up with renowned capital incinerator, Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) (and Oracle (ORCL)) on a supposed $500 billion dollar project. This is just silly, Softbank does not have nearly enough capital to fund even a portion of this, nor does OpenAI. Further Softbank is apparently in talks to invest in OpenAI at a reported ~$340 billion valuation, 2x their last value from just October! So yeah now I’m feeling more like its 1999.

It is obviously extremely difficult to predict something like this, and my view here is not strongly held. I do believe tech is the most important non political swing macro-economic driver though and as such bears close watching.

Ex tech, the forward momentum of the economy was looking fairly shaky for much of 2024, but has improved steadily over the last few months. As I mentioned previously, the Sahm rule (relating to increase in unemployment rate) was breached, and unemployment shot up pretty quickly earlier in the year. Thankfully it has since come down, and with the last jobs data we got on the 10th the recent trend is looking like perhaps it has stabilized.

Interest Rates & Valuations

Moving on to the other big piece of the puzzle, valuations and the cost of funds. On that front, the market had felt fairly to highly valued to me, but with the recent selloff some things are beginning to look more interesting. REITs in particular feel more fairly valued than over-valued, but with notable exceptions in both directions.

The biggest story, at least as relates to CRE, is probably the recent spike in interest rates.

The 10 year is up about 90 bps from its low in the fall, down slightly from over 100 a few weeks ago. This is obviously bad for financing costs, and all the myriad industries dependent on it (most notably real estate, but really anything hard asset driven). It also is not great for generic valuations across any investment asset, as the risk free return is higher.8

What is driving the increase? It doesn’t appear to be inflation, at least to me. Inflation had ticked back up in the fall, but came down significantly in the December and January data.

Source: Jason Furman on Twitter, give him a follow @Jasonfurman

Unlike last year, treasuries don’t seem to be moving much on inflation releases anymore (eg a year ago when inflation was the big narrative we would have seen a big drop in rates from the recent very low PCE inflation release). We saw a bit of easing with the PCI and CPI, but not as dramatic as last year’s moves.

My best guess is the market is pricing in higher inflation & supply disruptions due to potential Trump tariffs and also continued large fiscal deficit spending by the US govt (and indeed all western governments). This is also showing up in an increase in government bond yields across the west.9 This combination of fiscal deficits and supply disruption is a potent, potentially stagflationary brew. Thus, the return of the bond vigilante.

There is much talk of cost cutting with the new DOGE commission, but unfortunately the only way to really decrease government spending in a meaningful way is entitlement reform or to reduce military spending - non defense non entitlement spending is something like only 15% of federal spending I believe. And it doesn’t seem like anyone is ready to have that conversation yet, so continued large deficits seem very likely unfortunately.

As always though predicting the future is very difficult, and I do not have a high degree of confidence for how 2025 will shake out. Trump is already moving to impose high tariffs on Mexico, Canada and China - if the tariffs remain at current levels they could prove quite disruptive and create a huge drag on economic growth. At current levels the tariffs would be ~1% of GDP, which doesn’t account for the likely retaliatory tariffs we will see in response. The tariffs look very bad, but Trump has already within just a few days seemingly reversed course on the Mexican tariffs and promised to delay them for a month. The whole situation frankly is just pure chaos.10

I believe this means that the general economic growth/demand path has a pretty wide potential variance in outcomes, which calls for caution. Tech capex could continue to be a strong driver, or it could begin to unwind11. Small business optimism is up significantly, which could be a potent tailwind. Tariffs could range from relatively benign to disastrous. Given relatively high market valuations this is not an attractive combination.

Reflections on 2024

In keeping with tradition, I also try to look back at the prior year and reflect on what went well or poorly in my investments. We outperformed the REIT index which is nice, but were well below the S&P. I can’t control the S&P, so really all I can do is look at my own investments and what dragged my performance down. And the main drags on my portfolio were as follows.

First, the largest drag. I have a few small cap investments which declined despite the overall rise in CRE pricing & REITs. Generally speaking I still feel decent on these, although one which is quite small and lacks a specific catalyst I am feeling less good about. The other, ILPT, has a potential catalyst in the form of loans coming due in 2 years, which should force some kind of action that will hopefully de-lever the firm (or even trigger a sale). Although it is entirely possible they are able to refi the portfolio as is and just continue to scrape by while sweeping all free cash flow into management fees (the worst case scenario).

I think my biggest mistake here was investing as much in these while other larger, more mainstream investments were also quite cheap. I did this somewhat in the name of diversification, least I become too concentrated in a few stocks.

I think though given my high confidence levels in the more mainstream bets (VNO , MAC etc), in hindsight I should have put less in the small caps and even more in those, diversification be damned12. And then I could have saved the small hairy investments for later in the cycle (or not at all).

The other drag was weakness in Park Hotels & hospitality in general. There were some operational hiccups here (notably a strike in Hawaii, but also a general leisure slowdown), but generally I still feel confident in this investment and think it is quite cheap at current prices. Cash flow is strong and should grow moving forward. So I don’t think I really made any mistakes here… at least not yet.

Looking Forward

Looking into 2025 I still feel like the uncertain macro picture, combined with higher valuations, doesn’t feel like the best risk reward combination, but it is better than it was a few months ago.

Probably the biggest change for 2025 is I intend to look at some non real estate investments. I originally made some non RE investments back in 2022 when real estate prices were high and there were several non RE firms I thought were very attractive.

I decided to close these out in the hopes of presenting a cleaner real estate only focus that I was told would make a better story for institutional investors. Well, I was not able to raise any institutional capital, and selling these investments in fact dragged my returns down.13 My biggest of these was Spotify (SPOT), which operationally worked out essentially exactly as I thought it would.

So sad to have sold in 2022

Not every non CRE investment I made worked out well, but most did, several in a big way. A&F was another. The biggest loser has been Sportsman’s Warehouse (SPWH) - I still own a bit personally, and it has been painful.

I have decided that doing something for the sake of optics for a prospective LP is foolish given the difficulty in raising institutional LP capital anyways. So the goal now is to find the most attractive investments, where ever they may be. That said, real estate will remain a major focus as I know it best, and the caliber of competition there is pretty low.14 In fact I have spent much of the new year working on a CRE investment which has quickly become my largest position - I hope to do a detailed dive on this soon if time allows.

My approach to non real estate investments is ultimately similar to real estate. What is the current yield the investment can generate, and what are its growth prospects?15 Then how does this compare to other options, as well as what are the downside risks. The key difference is obviously understanding the operational & demand nuances of different industries, and so here I generally try to stick to things I understand well, or am able to learn relatively quickly. This covers software firms16, consumer goods or retail that I actually consume (or have good knowledge of), and asset heavy businesses among others.

I firmly believe that being a good investor is a general skill, and that it can be transferred across domains with sufficient base knowledge.

Fin

As always, thank you for reading. I am hopeful I will be able to continue generating strong returns for my investors in 2025 and beyond.

Footnotes

1 Although AI VC funding is also pretty hot.

2 I would also include the model training costs in capex as well, which while large don’t seem close to the chip spending itself.

3 And the model training spending is even worse, as they seem to become obsolete in a year or two after release.

4 I suspect end margins are lower, more in the 10-20% range, given that unlike traditional software LLMs have a marginal cost for each use, and traditional software margins seem to top out around 30% (with exceptions of course).

5 Unfortunately also it looks like the capex side is also set to dramatically increase in 2025, so it isn’t even clear if end use spending will begin to catch up much!

6 I suspect Deepseek isn’t able to perfectly take market share due to some firm’s hesitance to use Chinese software & switching inertia. However people are already working on getting versions up and running on US servers - this is the beauty of open source.

7 I think eventually revenues could hit those levels - in many years. Unfortunately those future returns would be to the benefit of future capex spending - the current spend seems almost certainly underwater. In investing getting the big trend right is the (relatively) easy part - its the timing that is hard!

8 Although as I have argued before, this is typically a short run impact and medium to long term quality hard assets or firms with pricing power should be an inflation hedge. The real issue is if rates rise without commensurately higher GDP growth, the much feared stagflation scenario. I don’t think fiscal deficits would be stagflationary by themselves (that is just pure vanilla inflation), but I do think high tariffs could be as they would effectively destroy/distort supply. A trade war is really a truly nasty economic risk as it just generates pure deadweight loss all around.

9 Interestingly China’s have fallen off a cliff recently, and are basically at all time lows.

10 I am becoming more worried large tariffs will stick around in some fashion. Trump really seems to be a mercantilist or even an autarkist… he has written and spoken extensively on his dislike of trade deficits & love of tariffs, and at this point I’m inclined to take him at his word. This subject could warrant its own article but I really do worry a trade war could have significant negative economic consequences.

11 At this point I am confident it will unwind, the question is just how soon. I’d guess 2026 at the latest, but it may happen this year.

12 The older I get the more I think diversification is a false god, if you have really quality highly compelling potential investments (which are not common). In which case just load up on the good stuff. But in a more unclear, richly valued environment I think diversification has more value, and I certainly think for the average investor diversification is very good because they simply lack the ability to properly evaluate when something truly is a phenomenal opportunity worth of heavy concentration.

13 Assuming I held to today’s pricing, which of course is hard to truly evaluate in retrospect.

14 Although the more I read from major hedge funds & tech investors the more it feels like the caliber of even large investors is just very low throughout the investing world.

15 As most are aware, the significantly higher potential growth rate in other industries requires a bit of a different mindset from value investing. But ultimately I do believe being a good real estate investor requires a lot of focus and attention on the growth rate there as well - things are a bit more transferable than they may seem from the outside looking in.

16 I actually know how to code and toyed around with building a CRE software startup years ago. I was for awhile hesitant here assuming a greater level of competence across the tech investment world but the more VCs I have met the more I realized that many people in tech are in fact very non technical, with pretty poor understanding of some of their own fundamentals. A lot of the tech investment world appears to have just been people in the right place at the right time, which really shouldn’t be so surprising given the astronomical returns some firms have generated for early investors. Was every early Google (GOOG, GOOGL) or Meta (META) investor a genius? A few probably were, but most were likely just average people who happened to have some personal connection to the founders and enough money at the time to invest.

Disclosure: This is not investing advice and please read the general disclaimer, here.

data.title: Open The Door To Owens Corning's Upside Potential
data.description:
Navigating the market noise requires a disciplined approach. By digging into the underlying financials, investors can uncover the true value of a stock – an essential skill for those looking to weather volatility, particularly with dividend-paying investments.

A particularly risky class of dividend stocks is those that offer decent yields and are backed by fundamentally sound businesses, yet trade at valuations that far exceed their true economic worth. These “dividend-trap” stocks carry extra risk with limited upside potential.

Our latest Long Idea is the opposite of a trap. This stock is positioned to benefit from the aging housing market and growth in its core markets. The company has strong fundamentals, returns ample capital to shareholders, and best of all, its stock is cheap.

We originally made Owens Corning (NYSE:OC) a Long Idea in January 2022 and most recently reiterated our thesis in July 2024. The stock has outperformed the S&P 500 since our original report. Even so, our thesis on this stock remains intact and additional upside potential remains.

OC offers favorable Risk/Reward based on the company’s:

  • position to benefit from an aging housing market,
  • growing core markets,
  • strong fundamentals and shareholder return,
  • quality executive compensation, and
  • cheap stock valuation.

What’s Working

Benefit From the Aging Housing Market

One of the most significant industry tailwinds driving Owens Corning is the aging housing stock in the U.S. The typical home hit a record age of 36 years old in 2024, which is up from 27 years in 2012. This increase in age of homes is due to the lack of new supply (fewer homes being built) and the better affordability of older homes.

The U.S. has been building fewer homes since the construction industry took a hit in 2008. Only 9% of U.S. homes were built in the 2010’s, which represents the lowest share of any one decade since the 1940’s.

While Owens Corning can sell products for new construction, the rising age of homes creates even more demand for Owens Corning’s remodel and repair products, such as roofing, insulation, and doors.

Figure 1: Median Age of U.S. Homes: 2012 – 2024

Source: Redfin

Growing Markets Provides Long-Term Opportunities

On February 14, 2025, Owens Corning signed a definitive agreement to sell its glass reinforcement business (previously reported in the Composites segment) to Praana Group. The sale completes the company’s strategic review of the business and focuses resources into the residential and commercial building products markets in North America and Europe.

As part of this strategic review, Owens Corning now has three reportable business segments: Roofing, Insulation, and Doors. Best of all, each of these three markets are forecasted to grow through the next decade:

  • The global roofing market is forecast to grow 4.3% compounded annually through 2034.
  • The global insulation market is forecast to grow 7.7% compounded annually through 2034.
  • The global doors market is forecast to grow 4.6% compounded annually through 2034.

As an existing industry leader, Owens Corning is in a great position to take advantage of the growth in each of the segments. Importantly, Owens Corning is not dependent on any one of these markets, as its business is well diversified across the three.

1Q25, Owens Corning generated 44% of its revenue from its roofing business, 35% from its insulation business, and 21% from its doors business. See Figure 2.

Figure 2: Owens Corning’s Revenue by Segment in 1Q25

Strong Fundamentals Across Decades

Owens Corning has grown revenue and net operating profit after-tax (NOPAT) by 8% and 17% compounded annually since 2014, respectively. See Figure 3.

The company improved its NOPAT margin from 6% in 2014 to 14% in the TTM while invested capital turns remained the same at 0.8 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins drive return on invested capital (ROIC) from 4% in 2014 to 11% in the TTM.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings grew 20% compounded annually from $209 million in 2014 to $1.4 billion in the TTM.

Figure 3: Owens Corning’s Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Executives Are Aligned with Shareholders

Corporate governance plays a crucial role in finding good stocks because it shows us how well executives’ interests are aligned with shareholders’ interests. By evaluating executive compensation plans, we can identify companies with quality corporate governance, such as Owens Corning.

Owens Corning’s executive compensation plan aligns the interests of executives and shareholders by tying one third of its long-term performance share awards to return on capital (ROC), a variation of ROIC.

As we mentioned in past reports, there is a strong correlation between improving ROIC and increasing shareholder value. Even though the company’s executive compensation plans could be improved by increasing the weighting of ROC or using ROIC as the main performance goal, the company’s inclusion of ROC, as a performance goal has helped create shareholder value by driving higher ROIC and economic earnings.

Per Figure 4, Owens Corning’s ROIC has increased from 4% in 2014 to 11% in the TTM. Economic earnings rose from -$104 million to $409 million over the same time.

Figure 4: Owens Corning’s ROIC and Economic Earnings Since 2014

Attractive Dividend and Repurchase Yield of 5%+

Since 2019, Owens Corning has paid $898 million (8% of market cap) in cumulative dividends and has increased its quarterly dividend from $0.22/share in 1Q19 to $0.69/share in 1Q25. The company’s current dividend, when annualized, provides a 1.9% yield.

Owens Corning also returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases. From 2019 through 1Q25, the company repurchased $2.8 billion (24% of market cap) worth of shares.

In May 2025, Owens Corning’s board of directors authorized a new 12 million share repurchase program. With the 5.7 million shares remaining from the previous authorization, the company currently is authorized to repurchase a total of 17.7 million shares.

Should the company repurchase shares at its TTM rate, it would repurchase $414 million of shares over the next year, which equals 3.5% of the current market cap. When combined, the dividend and share repurchase yield could reach 5.4%.

Strong Cash Flow Generation

We believe Owens Corning will be able to afford to pay its dividends and repurchase shares because of its large free cash flow (FCF). From 2019 through 2023, Owens corning generated $4.6 billion in FCF, which equals 25% of the company’s enterprise value.

The company completed its acquisition of Masonite in 2Q24, which led to FCF of -$3.7 billion in the quarter.

In each quarter since the acquisition (3Q24, 4Q24, and 1Q25), Owens Corning’s free cash flow has been positive and rising quarter-over-quarter (QoQ). Given the company’s consistent margins and steadily growing markets, we would expect free cash flow generation to continue rising in the future.

Overall, even with the acquisition, Owens Corning generated a cumulative $2.6 billion in FCF from 2019-1Q25. See Figure 5.

Figure 5: Owens Corning’s Cumulative FCF: 2019 – TTM

Owens Corning’s past repurchases have also meaningfully reduced its shares outstanding from 109 million in 2019 to 85 million at the end of 1Q25. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: Owens Corning’s Shares Outstanding: 2019 – 1Q25

Great Margins

Owens Corning is vertically integrated across each of its business segments. This integration creates efficiencies that manifest in consistently high profit margins.

Per Figure 7, Owens Corning has the second highest NOPAT margin among its competitors, which include Carlisle Companies (CSL), Cabot Corp (CBT), Dupont De Nemours (DD), and Hunstman Corp (HUN).

Figure 7: Owens Corning’ Profitability Vs. Peers: TTM

What’s Not Working

Slowdown in Home Sales

The current economic uncertainty is hurting consumer spending, especially when it comes to home purchases. According to Redfin, homes are selling at the slowest pace in the last six years, as demand is waning and homes remain expensive in the eyes of potential homebuyers. Additionally, $698 billion worth of homes are currently for sale, which represents an all-time high and an increase of 20% from a year ago.

A freezing of the home sale market could lead to (1) consumers slowing remodel plans, as sellers don’t want to spend on a home they plan to sell or (2) cautious owners who want to delay a remodel until the economy improves.

Management alluded to the cautious mindset of consumers in the company’s 1Q25 earnings call when they noted residential remodel activity is expected to remain weaker through the first half of the year.

However, while activity might be weaker in the short-term, the good news is the leading indicator of remodeling activity (LIRA) projects that remodeling activity in the U.S. will increase by 2.5% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.

Additionally, even if remodeling activity remains sluggish longer than forecasted, any potential downside to Owens Corning’s business is already priced into its current stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Permanent Profit Decline

At its current price of $140/share, OC has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio of 0.9. This ratio means the market expects the company’s profits to permanently decline 10% from TTM levels. For context, Owens Corning has grown NOPAT by 19% compounded annually over the last five years and 17% compounded annually over the last decade.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to analyze expectations for different stock price scenarios for OC.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 11% (below five-year average of 13% and TTM NOPAT margin of 14%) through 2034, and
  • revenue grows 4% a year through 2034 (compared to 9% CAGR over the last five years and 8% CAGR over the last decade) then

the stock would be worth $140/share today – nearly equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, revenue grows at the low end of the company’s end-market forecast growth. Additionally, Owens Corning’s NOPAT would grow just 1% compounded annually from 2025 – 2034, which is far below the company’s historical NOPAT growth rates.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Profits Grow Far Slower Than Historical Rates

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin falls to 12% (still below five-year average of 13% and TTM NOPAT margin of 14%) through 2034,
  • revenue grows 4% a year through 2034 (compared to 9% CAGR over the last five years and 8% CAGR over the last decade) then

the stock would be worth $171/share today – a ~20% upside to the current price. In this scenario, Owens Corning’s NOPAT would grow just 2% compounded annually through fiscal 2034, which would still be well below the company’s historical growth rates dating back to 2007. Should NOPAT grow closer to historical rates, the upside is even larger.

Figure 8 compares Owens Corning’s historical NOPAT to the NOPAT implied in each of the above scenarios.

Figure 8: Owens Corning’s Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

This article was originally published on June 11, 2025.

data.title: Argentine Banks: Volatility Is Not A Symptom Of Weakness, But Part Of The Ecosystem
data.description:
Investment Thesis: pause, not retreat

For months, Argentine banks listed on the NYSE were protagonists of one of the most intense rallies in the emerging universe.

Since the arrival of Javier Milei to power and the expectation of a sustained macroeconomic normalization, stocks such as Banco Macro (BMA), BBVA Argentina (BBAR), Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) and Grupo Supervielle (SUPV), which I have previously analyzed, multiplied their value in dollars, recovering levels that seemed impossible just a year ago.

But in July, the tide turned. Without warning, and without companies reporting substantial changes in their results, the market began to punish the sector with force. For those who do not follow the Argentine case closely, the fall may seem a symptom of deterioration. For those who know the cyclical history of their economy, this correction is almost a compulsory chapter within the same book: that of structural volatility.

In this scenario, I believe the most prudent option is to hold assets while waiting for new signals.

What happened? A combination of noisy signals that amplified perceived risk

The fall in Argentine bank stocks in July did not respond to a deterioration in the sector's operating fundamentals. There were no signs of an increase in late payment, no significant contraction in financial margins, and not even a substantial drop in quarterly results. Indeed, banks maintain healthy levels of capitalization, efficiency and liquidity. The abrupt change in market mood responded, instead, to a sequence of exogenous events that generated noise and uncertainty.

The first of these events was the alleged government intervention in the FX market. After months of an orthodox liberal discourse, in which the government had dismantled traps, cut monetary issuance and accumulated reserves, reports began to circulate that the Central Bank would have intervened to contain the financial dollar's price through covert purchases. This measure, interpreted by many as a contradiction of the announced course, triggered alarms among local and international investors.

Was it a technical correction to moderate weight appreciation? Or a symptom that internal tensions were forcing a shift in economic policy?

This was supplemented by technical market factors. After an exceptional rally in banking ADRs-with surges of over 300% in 12 months-, many institutional investors decided to take profits at the first sign of ambiguity. In a low-liquidity market such as the Argentine one, this rebalancing can be rapidly amplified. The volume traded in July showed simultaneous outflows from several funds, accelerating the correction without a clear signal from corporate balance sheets.

Finally, the impact of the political context cannot be ignored. The hardening of the conflict between the executive and some actors in Congress, the judicialization of key reforms and the increase in social unrest in certain sectors, fueled the perception that the cycle of reforms could not be sustained with the same strength. Institutional uncertainty, although not new in Argentina, is re-quoted every time political noise exceeds a certain threshold.

Taken together, these elements generated a dissonance between solid banking fundamentals and a country risk narrative that re-dominated asset prices. The correction, therefore, is not a reflection of structural weakness of the financial system, but of an external environment that -as so often in Argentina- acts as a catalyst for volatility.

An extraordinary rally followed by a logical correction

At the same time, the Argentine banks were coming off a meteoric rally. Since the lows of 2022, their dollar prices had multiplied by three, four or even more, in a context of reversal of expectations, risk compression and strong inflow of speculative flows after the political turn of December 2023. In 2025, the accumulated progress is still remarkable: BMA up +48.5%, GGAL +51%, BBAR +34.1% and SUPV +42.8%, despite having corrected between 5% and 7% during July.

This technical setback comes after a period of strong valuation improvements, with multiples still very low in relation to the ROE and the operating flow generated. SUPV, for example, is traded with a P/E FWD of just 4.4x and a P/B of 0.66x, while holding an ROE of 13.9% and a net margin above 12%. BMA, for its part, shows a projected ROE of 17% with a P/E of only 4.9x, well below the sector average in emerging markets, while maintaining capital ratios above 30% and an extremely efficient operating structure.

Even banks like BBAR, which corrected more strongly in the short term, show strong metrics: a P/E of 4.6x, an ROE of 15.5% and an ROE above 2.3%, along with a CFO that grew more than 60% year-on-year. In parallel, GGAL, which came to have one of the best momentum in the group, retains an ROE above 18%, a P/E FWD of 5.2x, and a controlled CAPEX that ensures efficiency amid uncertainty.

From a market point of view, the July correction is not surprising when looking at the momentum metrics in decline: BBAR and SUPV were rated "F" in performance at 3 and 6 months, with declines of between 30% and 45% in those periods. BMA, which also displays a note "F" at 3 and 6 months, maintains a positive performance in the year, although slower. This technical dynamic reflects a partial rotation of flows after the rally, rather than a change in fundamentals.

Valuation, growth and profitability: solid foundations in papers still punished

Beyond the technical adjustment observed in July, the financial fundamentals of Argentine banks continue to show signs of strength on three key fronts: attractive valuation multiples, sustained operational growth rate and high profitability against their share price. This triad is precisely the core of the thesis that continues to justify interest in the sector, even under scenarios of political noise or tactical correction.

From a valuation perspective, all banks analyzed are quoted with multiples significantly lower than their emerging market peers. With a P/E FWD of 4.9x and a P/B of 1.15x, BMA leads in efficiency without falling into overvaluation. GGAL, with a P/E of 5.2x and an ROE of 18%, offers one of the most interesting risk-return ratios in the sector.

SUPV continues to be the cheapest bank by multiples: quoted at just 3.9x earnings and 0.72x book value, while retaining a net margin of 12.3% and an ROE close to 14%. BBAR, with 4.6x P/E and 0.88x P/B, combines its attractiveness with an ROA of 2.3% and strong YoY growth in operating cash flow (+62.7%).

From the growth angle, all four banks show positive rates in EPS projections, led by BBAR (+50.8%) and BMA (+44.2%), reflecting not only the recovery of the credit business but also a substantial improvement in efficiency. SUPV and GGAL also project strong gains (+35.6% and +28.3% respectively), which, combined with low multiples, suggests room for price expansion.

In terms of profitability, ROE is the key metric for measuring efficiency: BMA and GGAL remain over 17% and SUPV and BBAR also exceed 13%. If you add the return on dividends (between 2.4% and 3.5% annualized), the total carry becomes relevant, especially in a positive real rate environment like the current one.

Macro environment: stabilization with no guarantee of continuity

At the macroeconomic level, the stabilization program achieved incredible progress: monthly inflation reduced to 1.5% in May, the lowest level in more than 5 years, and in June it was 1.6% (+39.4% YoY). Meanwhile, international reserves continue to be restocked.

However, these improvements were realized under exceptional conditions-contractive policy and capital controls still in place-leaving open the possibility of reversal if institutional confidence is not consolidated.

Political risk is once again at the front in the run-up to the legislative elections in October. Everything is in pause waiting for the results: the fragility of the parliamentary support, the social resistance to certain reforms and the possibility of institutional paralysis again put price on the uncertainty, even when the bank balances show solidity. A triumph of officialdom would allow it to advance into a second phase of deeper reforms, for which it needs more allies in Congress.

Conclusion: tactical pause, strategic opportunity

What is happening with the Argentine banks is not a collapse, but a pause. A noisy pause, yes. But I do not think it is a sign of structural weakness.

For the moment I consider that the best option is to hold, since in Argentina there is no prize without a shock. This has always been the case, and will continue to be so as long as political institutions do not accompany them with the same strength that these banks have silently and resiliently demonstrated.

data.title: WTI Crude Price Steadies Near $66 As Inventory Draw And Trade Hopes Ease Selling Pressure (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil futures are trading near $65.79 as of Thursday afternoon, recovering from a recent four-session decline. The modest rebound comes amid signs of easing trade tensions between the U.S. and European Union, alongside stronger-than-expected inventory data from the Energy Information Administration.

However, the broader price trend remains constrained below key resistance levels as traders await further clarity on global demand and macroeconomic developments. According to Wednesday’s EIA report, U.S. crude stockpiles fell by 3.2 million barrels, significantly more than the 2 million expected.

Gasoline inventories also declined by 1.7 million barrels, while distillates posted a 2.9 million barrel build. The net draw from crude and gasoline is seen as a bullish signal, reflecting solid end-user consumption, even as distillates suggest some downstream caution.

Price range holds as market eyes next catalyst

Technically, WTI crude remains within a well-defined range between $63.50 and $68.00, unable to decisively break either boundary. The price has found interim support near the lower bound of this zone, but resistance looms from the tightly packed 20/50/100/200 EMAs, which stretch from $66.07 to $68.12. Momentum is muted, with RSI (14) hovering at 46.81 and still below the neutral line.

Traders are also closely watching trade developments. U.S.–EU negotiations are reportedly advancing toward a deal that would reduce tariff threats on European imports. Meanwhile, upcoming discussions between U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Chinese officials may influence global oil flows, especially if agreements emerge regarding China’s sourcing from sanctioned suppliers.

Outlook remains neutral until breakout above $68.50

For now, WTI crude remains trapped in a neutral-to-bearish technical structure, with only a break above $68.50 likely to trigger a broader trend reversal toward $72–$73. Downside risk persists if $63 fails to hold. Continued clarity on global trade, inventory trends, and Chinese demand will be key in determining the next leg for oil markets.

In our earlier outlooks, we highlighted the $63–$68 range as the key battleground for WTI crude prices. The current bounce off the lower edge follows the anticipated inventory-driven pivot, but broader direction will require resolution of trade-linked supply routes and a breach of EMA resistance.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

data.title: Logitech: Resilient And Strategically Positioned For Sustainable Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

Logitech (NASDAQ:LOGI) is very well positioned, led by growth opportunities in its core markets (productivity peripherals, gaming, and video conferencing) and sound fundamentals with a balanced exposure in B2C (~60%) and B2B (~40%) markets. Their continuous effort towards product innovation & launches, and resilient consumer demand (despite pricing actions) reflects their brand reputation. On margin performance, despite global macroeconomic uncertainty and tariff headwinds, the company capitalizes on recent price action (reflecting strong consumer and enterprise brand equity), growth in core segments like gaming and video collaboration, and effective supply chain diversification. Logitech executes disciplined strategies, innovates new products, and proactively mitigates tariffs, suggesting that long-term growth remains intact. Although for the time being, the company has temporarily withdrawn FY26 guidance, I believe a return to formal FY guidance post-tariff stabilization could act as a near-term catalyst.

While some near-term headwinds are present here, a lot of that pessimism is already reflected by the stock’s current valuation. The growth story is considered compelling, and with the current valuation, the stock is currently trading at below its 5-year average forward P/E, which can be a potential entry point. Paired with solid fundamentals, brand value, cost measures, and execution, room for upside is definitely present.

Company Overview

Logitech is a more then 4-decade-old company with its roots from 1981 and is headquartered in Lausanne, Switzerland. LOGI designs and manufactures peripherals that allow people to connect, including keyboards, mice, webcams, video conferencing, gaming devices, keyboards and combos, pointing devices, webcams, headsets, and tablet accessories. The company earns 60% of its Revenue from B2C and 40% from B2B (enterprise) customers. In FY24, Logi derives ~44% revenue from the Americas, ~30% from EMEA, and ~25% from APAC. The company operates a highly diversified supply chain spanning six countries and continues to invest in AI-enabled products, design-led innovation, and operational efficiency. They operate under multiple brands, including Logitech, Jaybird, Logitech G, Blue, Astro, and Ultimate Ears.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Logitech delivered FY25 net sales of $4.55bn, growth of 6% YoY basis and 7% YoY growth on a constant currency basis. The Q4 FY25 revenue was $1.01bn, almost flat on YoY basis but witnessed a ~2% YoY growth on a constant currency basis, which shows stability despite ongoing macro pressures and tariff headwinds.

I believe Logitech’s B2C segment (which is 60%) will remain robust with consistent sell-through momentum led by the company's product superiority and brand reputation. An uptick in demand for more cost-effective entertainment is being provided by the current weakened macro environment, especially in countries such as China, with gaming being at the top of that list. This is being used by management to their advantage in conjunction with proper segmentation, innovation, and partnerships to facilitate demand growth in gaming. B2B demand, especially in video collaboration, continues to benefit from hybrid work adoption. However, enterprise sentiment remains cautious; however, it is noted by management that an opportunity to monetize on the demand for enterprises looking for more cost-effective means of video conferencing is provided by the current environment. The initiative of product superiority in this segment as a demand driver is further reinforced by the addition of AI products with video conferencing. And the company’s recent AI-enabled enterprise video conferencing products (Rally Bar, Rally Board, Sight, etc) are well-positioned for global expansion.

In FY25, Logitech presented a very bold story of innovation and design excellence. 39 new products were launched, delighting users with standout offerings like the Combo Touch Keyboard Case, the Pro X Superlight Gaming Mouse, and China’s top-selling Alto Keys Mechanical Keyboard. Sleek design was combined with software-enabled features in each product, earning global acclaim. This innovation push wasn’t just for show; market share and product categories were expanded by Logitech. A strategy rooted in understanding user needs and constant reinvention reinforced Logitech's identity as a global leader in smart, stylish peripherals.

Market expansion paired with resilient consumer demand should continue to be a tailwind. Logi already holds the #1-2 spot in 11 out of 13 categories where they compete, which shows their brand value. The company is also navigating the tarrif impact strategically, in the mid of April of this year, Logitech implemented a ~10% price increase in US products (from 0% to double-digit percentage increases), which may have some short-term impact, but management believes its strong brand and premium product positioning will support customer acceptance, so price elasticity doesn't look like a major risk here.

All in, I expect LOGI revenue to keep growing, backed by market share gains, steady innovation, and launches. International diversification remains a core revenue driver. With the majority (⅔)of sales outside the U.S., Logitech benefits from global exposure. Logi is witnessing early signs of success in their emerging markets, and I believe that with their deeper channel penetration in EMEA and Asia-Pacific, growth will be further fueled going forward.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Logitech’s profits are holding up well. Logitech's profitability metrics showed strong improvement in FY25. Adj. Gross margin inched up to 43.5% up by 170bps YoY, primarily driven by product cost reductions, value engineering, and better product mix. Adj. Operating income for the same period was $775mn with an operating margin of 17.0% vs 16.3% in FY24.

On the margin front, while tariffs are a headwind, the company is taking several steps to offset them. Gross margins should benefit from continued improvements in their innovation mix and the strategic price increases (~10% avg in the U.S. effective mid-April 2025), early inventory pull-forward, and supply chain optimization.

The company is expecting tariffs to pose a ~$50mn annualized headwind, but they're actively working on ways to soften the blow. Currently, of their total US sales, they source ~40% from China, which they are planning to bring down to merely ~10% by the end of CY25. That shift, along with ongoing cost-saving initiatives and supplier concessions, should help cushion the impact. Still, the tariffs will start hitting Q1 margins before the 2nd round pricing actions kick (the company has indicated the room for increase, but the magnitude and timing are in watch mode).

For the June quarter, management guided the net impact of tariffs to be ~200 bps (including the impact from price increases and cost-saving measures). Looking ahead, recent tariff changes have reduced the gross impact from tariffs from 500 bps to 300 bps, which is expected to be partially offset by ~200 bps of full quarter pricing changes, resulting in a net incremental impact of ~100 bps to gross margins level.

I expect, margin to improve over the medium term, led by positive growth in high-margin categories like video collaboration and AI-powered peripherals. Additionally, positive operating leverage should be provided by disciplined SG&A and ongoing operational efficiencies. The commitment to R&D and innovation was reiterated by management while a tight leash on G&A and discretionary spend is being maintained. Support for gross margin resilience will also be provided by continued value engineering and supply chain optimization.

All in all, I expect the margin to bottom out in Q1 and recover from there onward, with a good medium- to long-term expansion potential, as pricing actions and supply diversification fully materialize.

Peers and Differentiation:

Corsair (CRSR) : While also focusing on gaming peripherals, Corsair positions enthusiast-level products more narrowly. Logitech’s broader product suite and global retail partnerships provide it with greater scale and brand diversification.

HP (HPQ) : Select segments like mice and keyboards are competed in by HP, but Logitech’s dedicated consumer focus and innovation speed are lacking in HP. HP's peripheral business is only a small subset of HP's larger PC and printing ecosystem.

Razer (OTCPK:RAZFF) : Known for gaming hardware, Razer is recognized for its strong design and community engagement, but is operated at a premium pricing tier. Logitech’s strength is found in its ability to balance premium design with accessible pricing, supported by global channel reach.

Key Differentiators:

• Speed to market and innovation cadence (e.g., AI-enabled Rally Bar and Logitech G gaming suite)

• Diversified manufacturing and supply chain footprint

• Strong cash position and operational leverage

• Pricing power supported by brand loyalty

Overall, Logitech’s market positioning is strengthened by its consistent product innovation and supply chain flexibility, distribution scale, allowing market leadership to be maintained even in a fragmented and competitive environment.

Valuation:

Over the period, the company has successfully reduced its dependency on PC to drive its sales of peripherals to a diversified growth company focused on peripherals in gaming, video collaboration, and content creation products. Significant secular drivers underpin the growth of peripherals in these categories (streaming content creation, eSports, hybrid work and learn from home and casual gaming, and increased consumerization of video).

Product innovation remains a key edge. The company’s recent AI-enabled video conferencing products (Rally Bar, Rally Board, Sight, etc) are well received by the market for these high-ASP, higher-margin products.

I believe stock deserve a future re-rating likely on: i) consistent market share gains and ii) likely impact of increased topline in operating leverage. However, while there are some near-term questions related to margin play in, the longer-term worries seem manageable. Management consistently makes an effort to diversify sourcing, and price increases should be absorbed fairly well.

I am above consensus on the EPS forecast for both FY26e and FY27e. This is in contrast to previous guidance with revenues in the scope of $4.50bn- $4.7bn (or rev growth (CC) of 1%-5%) and operating income of $720- $780mn. It is worth noting that Logitech pulled its FY26 guidance on April 10 due to significant economic uncertainty, and I believe it will return to formal FY guidance post-tariff stabilization. Even in the absence of the same in the near term, I am impressed with the potential for share growth due to the company’s improved ability, especially with new launches, to navigate the tariff headwinds better than expected, paired with the ongoing momentum of operating leverage.
